front: Poem: 'The time you won your town the race / We chaired you through the market-place'; 'early though the laurel grows / It withers quicker than the rose'
back: "To an Athlete Dying Young" (A. E. Housman)

front: Poem: a wise man advises 'Give crowns and pounds and guineas / But not your heart away'
Poem: 'malt does more than Milton can / To justify God's ways to man'; ends 'Mithridates, he died old'
back: "Terence, This Is Stupid Stuff" (A. E. Housman)

front: Poem: a cherry tree 'hung with snow' / 'Wearing white for Eastertide'; 'fifty springs are little room' to see 'things in bloom'
back: "Loveliest of Trees, the Cherry Now" (A. E. Housman)
work: Loveliest of Trees, the Cherry Now

front: Poem: the speaker watches a gale and imagines 'the Roman and his trouble / are ashes under Uricon'
Poem: a dead man asks questions like 'is football playing along the river shore?'; his friend now 'cheers a dead man's sweetheart'
Author: this poet is the protagonist of Tom Stoppard's The Invention of Love, crossing the River Styx at age 77 while reflecting on his love for Moses Jackson
Author: classical scholar who spent thirty years editing Manilius's Astronomica, calling Manilius 'facile and frivolous'
Poem: a parody of Greek drama in which Eriphyle is told 'thy health is poor; But thine arithmetic is quite correct'
back: "Fragment of a Greek Tragedy" (A. E. Housman)

front: Poem: eulogizes mercenaries who 'saved the sum of things for pay'; 'what God abandoned, these defended'
back: "Epitaph on an Army of Mercenaries" (A. E. Housman)

front: Author: George Butterworth set eleven of this poet's works to music; Ralph Vaughan Williams composed On Wenlock Edge from six of his poems
